title: Darvas boxes: clear ceilings and floors

choices: A price range where the stock repeatedly hits similar highs and lows
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Draw a ceiling and a floor — price ping-pongs inside until it doesn’t.

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: A breakout without strong volume often:
choices: Is treated as suspect — demand may be weak
Is always better than high volume

teachSlides.title: Boxes reduce noise
teachSlides.body: Darvas looked for defined ranges. When price breaks the top with force, it can signal demand won. Until then, patience — rumors don’t replace price.

teachSlides.title: Volume as ignition
teachSlides.body: A spike in volume on a breakout can act like fuel — low-volume breakouts can fail more often (not always).

teachSlides.title: Stops follow price
teachSlides.body: The “ghost stop” idea: as price rises, raise your floor under the trade. If wrong, exit — don’t argue with the tape.

recapSlide.title: Recap
recapSlide.body: Define the box, demand volume on breakout, ignore noise, and trail risk. Same old story: process beats tips.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Price breaks out of a $50–$55 box to $56 on below-average volume. Many Darvas-style traders would:
choices: Treat it as weak confirmation — may wait or skip
masteryQuestions.explanation: Volume helps validate demand at the break.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Inside the box, rumors from “the street” are often:
choices: Secondary to what price and volume actually do
masteryQuestions.explanation: Price action is the vote that counts in this style.

choices: Lock in progress and cap downside if the trend breaks
masteryQuestions.explanation: They’re a risk tool, not a profit promise.
